summary:
The Warring States Period (475–221 BC) was a time of intense interstate conflict in ancient China, during which seven major states — Qin, Chu, Qi, Yan, Zhao, Wei, and Han — fought for dominance in a centuries-long struggle that ended only when the Qin state conquered all rivals and unified China under [[event:76|Qin Dynasty / Unification of China]].

The period followed the collapse of the Zhou dynasty's authority and was characterized by relentless warfare fought with new iron weapons and mass infantry armies that replaced the chariot warfare of earlier eras. States competed to attract talented administrators and military commanders, creating intense intellectual ferment — the [[event:1112|Hundred Schools of Thought]] flourished as philosophers debated how to achieve stable government, with Confucians, Legalists, Daoists, Mohists, and others all competing for royal patronage. Major military events included the catastrophic [[event:1118|Battle of Changping]] (260 BC) in which the state of Qin reportedly buried hundreds of thousands of Zhao prisoners. The period's incessant warfare paradoxically drove institutional innovation: states developed bureaucratic administration, standardized coinage, and professional armies that became the template for imperial China.

The ultimate victors were the Qin, whose brutal Legalist approach to statecraft and military organization proved most effective in the competition. Under [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]], who completed the [[event:1119|Qin Shi Huang Unification]] between 230 and 221 BC, China was united for the first time under a single emperor — a transformation that would shape Chinese civilization for over two millennia. The states absorbed by Qin had distinct cultures, languages, and traditions; their forced integration under standardized laws, writing, and measurements was as revolutionary as the military conquest itself.
